EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the sixth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits of 1 to 2 inches common during the month of January. Mean temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but below normal during December and January. While precipitation was below normal for the month, a disturbance brought almost an inch of rainfall west of I-55, which led to modest improvement in the drought conditions. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought expanded across central and eastern Illinois. Streamflows, lake levels, and regional water tables remained well below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a multi-month precipitation deficit dating back to the Fall of 2025, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. An early month disturbance brought almost an inch of rain, resulting in modest improvement in the drought by mid-January.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a multi-month precipitation deficit dating back to the Fall of 2025, Severe (D2) Drought was ongoing at the start of the month. The cooperative observer in Decatur measured 0.81 inches of precipitation during the month of January, which was 1.59 inches below normal. The continued below normal precipitation resulted in Severe Drought persisting through the month. Water levels on Lake Decatur were almost 4 feet below normal by the end of the month, and Stage 1 water rationing was implement for the city of Decatur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level disturbance interacting with a nearly stationary frontal boundary draped across the Deep South produced a prolonged overrunning snowfall event across central and southern Illinois from the morning of January 24th to the morning of January 25th.  ||By the time the snow tapered off and came to an end, significant amounts had been observed...especially along and south of the I-70 corridor where amounts of 8 to 12 inches were common.  Some of the highest totals were recorded across southern Richland County around Olney where a few reports indicated 13-14 inches.   Amounts decreased further north, with 4-8 inches along the I-72 corridor from Jacksonville to Danville...and just 1 to 3 inches along and north of a Macomb to Peoria line. Northeasterly winds gusting 20-25mph on January 24th and north-northwesterly winds gusting 25-30mph on January 25th created considerable drifting of the snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observers measured 5 to 7 inches of snow across Menard County.  Due to both falling and drifting snow, travel became extremely hazardous...leading to numerous traffic accidents on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant convection from Oklahoma pushed into the bi-state area and strengthened with the help of a potent low-level jet. These renewed thunderstorms were able to cause sporadic wind damage in south-central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused sporadic damage from Hillsboro northeastward to the Shelby/Montgomery/Christian county line. Municipalities include Irving, where a power pole was downed near McCords Trail and IL 16 along the Union Pacific railroad, Witt, Coalton, Nokomis, and Ohlman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant convection from Oklahoma pushed into the bi-state area and strengthened with the help of a potent low-level jet. These renewed thunderstorms were able to cause sporadic wind damage in south-central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds felled a tree across County Road 000E. This storm went on to cause damage in Macoupin County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed limbs in Shattuc.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of above normal temperatures through the first half of January, a series of cold fronts brought a significant pattern change and much colder conditions for the latter part of the month.  An Arctic cold front that pushed through central Illinois during the evening of January 22nd dropped temperatures below zero by the morning of January 23rd.  Due to the falling temperatures and brisk northwesterly winds gusting 25-30mph, wind chill values plummeted into the -25 to -35 degree range along and north of a Macomb to Bloomington line.  Despite the dangerously cold temperatures, no cold-related injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind-chill values of -25 to -35 occurred across Knox County during the morning of January 23rd. The coldest hourly wind-chill reading observed at the Galesburg Airport (KGBG) was -35.

Due to the falling temperatures and brisk northwesterly winds gusting 25-30mph, wind chill values plummeted into the -25 to -35 degree range along and north of a Macomb to Bloomington line.  Despite the dangerously cold temperatures, no cold-related injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind-chill values of -25 to -35 occurred across Peoria County during the morning of January 23rd. The coldest hourly wind-chill reading observed at the Peoria Airport (KPIA) was -28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level disturbance interacting with a nearly stationary frontal boundary draped across the Deep South produced a prolonged overrunning snowfall event across central and southern Illinois from the morning of January 24th to the morning of January 25th.  ||By the time the snow tapered off and came to an end, significant amounts had been observed...especially along and south of the I-70 corridor where amounts of 8 to 12 inches were common.  Some of the highest totals were recorded across southern Richland County around Olney where a few reports indicated 13-14 inches.   Amounts decreased further north, with 4-8 inches along the I-72 corridor from Jacksonville to Danville...and just 1 to 3 inches along and north of a Macomb to Peoria line. Northeasterly winds gusting 20-25mph on January 24th and north-northwesterly winds gusting 25-30mph on January 25th created considerable drifting of the snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observers measured 8 to 12 inches of snow across Jasper County...including 10.5 inches in Newton.  Due to both falling and drifting snow, travel became extremely hazardous...leading to numerous traffic accidents on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southern Illinois, followed by a ridge of high pressure that originated from Siberia, bringing prolonged sub-freezing temperatures beginning on the 23rd. Breezy northerly winds with gusts over 20 mph caused wind chills to plummet between -5 to -12 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours and continued into the morning of the 24th. Areas along and northwest of interstate 57 experienced another interval of dangerous wind chills during the morning and afternoon hours on the 25th while areas to the southeast remained warmer above advisory criteria when a major winter storm was ongoing.

A significant overrunning snow event unfolded across central and southeast Illinois from the afternoon of January 24th through the afternoon of January 25th.  Snowfall was heaviest along and south of the I-70 corridor where 8 to 14 inches accumulated.  Once the snow ended and skies cleared, air temperatures dropped below zero over the fresh snow cover by the morning of January 26th.  Due to west-northwest winds of 10 to 15mph, wind chill values bottomed out in the -20 to -30 degree range.  Despite the dangerously cold conditions, no cold-related injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind-chill values of -20 to -30 occurred across Scott County during the morning of January 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant overrunning snow event unfolded across central and southeast Illinois from the afternoon of January 24th through the afternoon of January 25th.  Snowfall was heaviest along and south of the I-70 corridor where 8 to 14 inches accumulated.  Once the snow ended and skies cleared, air temperatures dropped below zero over the fresh snow cover by the morning of January 26th.  Due to west-northwest winds of 10 to 15mph, wind chill values bottomed out in the -20 to -30 degree range.  Despite the dangerously cold conditions, no cold-related injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind-chill values of -20 to -30 occurred across Crawford County during the morning of January 26th. The coldest hourly wind-chill reading observed at the Robinson Airport (KRSV) was -25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged northward through central Illinois during the afternoon of January 8th, introducing unseasonably warm temperatures in the lower to middle 60s.  As low pressure and its associated cold front approached from the west, the pressure gradient tightened sufficiently to produce southwesterly winds of 20-30mph.  Gusts reached 45-55mph at times east of the I-55 corridor...with a peak measured gust of 60mph at the Coles County Airport.  Due to the gusty winds, minor wind damage was reported in Mattoon-Charleston in Coles County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 60mph was measured at the Coles County Airport at 8:37 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacting with an unseasonably warm early January airmass with temperatures in the lower to middle 60s triggered a couple lines of strong to severe thunderstorms during the evening of January 8th.  A few of the storms produced minor wind damage in Tazewell County and toppled two semis further east in Champaign County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four power poles were snapped along Stringtown Road just southwest of Minier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light wintry mix developed during the early morning hours of the 21st across portions of southern Illinois. Temperatures were highly variable across the region between 32-36 degrees, but colder ground temperatures allowed for areas of light freezing rain to develop before temperatures quickly warmed that day, resulting in slippery and icy conditions on roadways. Roads were particularly bad near Glendale in Pope County, IL. Ice on rural roadways in Saline County, IL caused one vehicle to overturn with a person trapped. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

For the sixth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits of 1 to 2 inches common during the month of January. Mean temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but below normal during December and January. While precipitation was below normal for the month, a disturbance brought almost an inch of rainfall west of I-55, which led to modest improvement in the drought conditions. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought expanded across central and eastern Illinois. Streamflows, lake levels, and regional water tables remained well below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a multi-month precipitation deficit dating back to the Fall of 2025, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought was ongoing at the start of the month. Continued below normal precipitation led to the Severe to Extreme drought persisting through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass with sub-freezing temperatures that originated from Siberia was reinforced by a major winter storm that brought a snowpack across portions of southern Illinois on the 25th. Breezy west to northwest winds with occasional gusts around 20 mph caused dangerous intervals of wind chills between -15 to locally -18 in Williamson County, IL on the 26th. Northwest portions of southern Illinois and areas south of route 13 only experienced advisory criteria with values of -9 to -14. Dangerous wind chills eventually came to an end during the early morning hours of the 27th. The one exception was in Alexander, Pulaski, and Massac County, IL where wind chills warmed above -5 the morning of the 26th due to calmer winds.

Scattered thunderstorms along with heavy rain moved across parts of northeast Illinois during the evening of January 8th, producing flash flooding and localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on numerous streets and roadways, including in Lemont, Homer Glen, Oak Lawn, Chicago Ridge and Hickory Hills.  In the City of Chicago, street flooding was reported in Washington Park and Auburn Gresham.  Basement flooding was reported in Chicago Lawn, Ashburn, West Englewood and Auburn Gresham.  Route 83 was flooded between Archer Avenue and Route 45.  Rainfall reports included 2.89 inches near Crestwood and 2.70 inches in Oak Lawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms along with heavy rain moved across parts of northeast Illinois during the evening of January 8th, producing flash flooding and localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in multiple areas in Wheeling, Prospect Heights and Mount Prospect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front moved through southern Illinois on the morning of the 30th, bringing cold temperatures down to the teens. Blustery northwest winds caused wind chills to fall around -5 to -7. Intervals of dangerous wind chills were variable across the region during the morning hours on the 30th and 31st with the far southern counties remaining above threshold.

A cold air mass with sub-freezing temperatures that originated from Siberia was reinforced by a major winter storm that brought a snowpack across portions of southern Illinois on the 25th. Breezy west to northwest winds with occasional gusts around 20 mph caused dangerous intervals of wind chills between -15 to locally -18 in Williamson County, IL on the 26th. Northwest portions of southern Illinois and areas south of route 13 only experienced advisory criteria with values of -9 to -14. Dangerous wind chills eventually came to an end during the early morning hours of the 27th. The one exception was in Alexander, Pulaski, and Massac County, IL where wind chills warmed above -5 the morning of the 26th due to calmer winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on numerous streets and roadways across central Cook County including in the City of Chicago.  Street flooding was reported in Burbank, Uptown, Lincoln Square, Albany Park, Irving Park, Near West Side, Garfield Park, Downtown Chicago and in Hyde Park. Basement flooding was reported in Belmont Gragin, Austin, Humboldt Park, West Garfield Park, Garfield Ridge, Clearing, Archer Heights, West Elsdon, West Lawn, Dunning, Portage Park and Irving Park.||Specific reports of flooding included a public transportation bus stuck in floodwaters near 47th Street and Wood Street.  The California Avenue underpass at Interstate 55 was flooded and impassable.  Ashland Avenue viaducts were flooded between 15th Street and 16th Street.  The Route 34 underpass east of Route 45 was closed due to flooding.  A water rescue was reported at a flooded underpass on Kedzie Avenue between Interstate 55 and 31st Street.  Multiple lanes of Interstate 90 were flooded between North Avenue and Division Street.  A vehicle was stalled in flood waters at Carroll Avenue and Sacramento Boulevard.||Rainfall reports included 3.37 inches near La Grange; 2.27 inches in Burbank and 2.25 inches near Brighton Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Illinois during the morning of January 14th with a band of heavy snow and snow squalls. Wind gusts were between 40 mph and 50 mph with a few higher gusts, including a gust to 61 mph near Crescent City. Visibility was reported to be near zero in many locations. Snowfall amounts were generally between 1 and 2 inches and with temperatures falling below freezing as the snow began, roads became icy.  Hundreds of vehicle accidents were reported across northern Illinois.

Snow developed across east central and northeast Illinois during the afternoon of January 24th and continued through the afternoon of January 25th.  Snowfall amounts were generally 2 to 4 inches through the Interstate 55 corridor and 4 to 6 inches along and east of the Interstate 57 corridor.  Lake enhanced snow developed on January 25th with higher snowfall amounts across eastern Cook County.  Snowfall amounts there included 10.5 inches in the Chicago Loop; 9.1 inches in Garfield Park; 9.0 inches in Avondale and Bridgeport; 8.8 inches in Glenview and 8.5 inches in Logan Square.  Chicago O'Hare Airport measured 4.4 inches.

A major winter storm impacted southern Illinois on the 24th with light to moderate snow developing during the morning hours. After a brief lull in the evening, moderate to heavy snow developed with a second wave that continued overnight into the morning hours of the 25th. Along and south of Route 13 towards Interstate 24, a brief period of sleet had occurred as above freezing temperatures aloft advected north. Colder air wrapped around on the backside, allowing for precipitation to transition back to all snow before ending during the afternoon hours. Between 8 to 14 inches of snow and sleet was observed with the lowest totals occurring in areas that experienced mixing. ||Significant travel impacts occurred with snow instantly accumulating on all surfaces due to temperatures only in the upper single digits and teens during the bulk of the event. Roads became impassable closer to the Ohio River where 1 to 2 inches of sleet led to a very dense accumulation on road surfaces and the worst driving conditions in 15 to 20 years in Massac County, IL. Route 3, 127, and 146 in Alexander County, IL were all snow and ice covered, which led to the County Sheriff issuing an Emergency Travel Only Order due to all roads being too dangerous for travel. Interstate 57 in Williamson County, IL also experienced very difficult travel due to the snow while Jefferson County, IL near Mount Vernon had a multi-vehicle pileup that blocked the southbound side during the afternoon hours on the 25th.  The collision near milepost 92 involved seven vehicles, including a jackknifed truck-tractor semi-trailer that led to minor injures and traffic being diverted. Wind gusts around 20 to 25 mph led to blowing and drifting snow, especially near Du Quoin in Perry County, IL where most secondary roads were also impassable. The extreme cold after the event hampered clean-up efforts and made snow removal extremely difficult. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, government closures.

The extreme cold after the event hampered clean-up efforts and made snow removal extremely difficult. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, road closures, government closures.

A major winter storm impacted southern Illinois on the 24th with light to moderate snow developing during the morning hours. After a brief lull in the evening, moderate to heavy snow developed with a second wave that continued overnight into the morning hours of the 25th. Along and south of Route 13 towards Interstate 24, a brief period of sleet had occurred as above freezing temperatures aloft advected north. Colder air wrapped around on the backside, allowing for precipitation to transition back to all snow before ending during the afternoon hours. Between 8 to 14 inches of snow and sleet was observed with the lowest totals occurring in areas that experienced mixing. ||Significant travel impacts occurred with snow instantly accumulating on all surfaces due to temperatures only in the upper single digits and teens during the bulk of the event. Roads became impassable closer to the Ohio River where 1 to 2 inches of sleet led to a very dense accumulation on road surfaces and the worst driving conditions in 15 to 20 years in Massac County, IL. Route 3, 127, and 146 in Alexander County, IL were all snow and ice covered, which led to the County Sheriff issuing an Emergency Travel Only Order due to all roads being too dangerous for travel. Interstate 57 in Williamson County, IL also experienced very difficult travel due to the snow while Jefferson County, IL near Mount Vernon had a multi-vehicle pileup that blocked the southbound side during the afternoon hours on the 25th. Wind gusts around 20 to 25 mph led to blowing and drifting snow, especially near Du Quoin in Perry County, IL where most secondary roads were also impassable. Snow developed across east central and northeast Illinois during the afternoon of January 24th and continued through the afternoon of January 25th.  Snowfall amounts were generally 2 to 4 inches through the Interstate 55 corridor and 4 to 6 inches along and east of the Interstate 57 corridor.  Lake enhanced snow developed on January 25th with higher snowfall amounts across eastern Cook County.  Snowfall amounts there included 10.5 inches in the Chicago Loop; 9.1 inches in Garfield Park; 9.0 inches in Avondale and Bridgeport; 8.8 inches in Glenview and 8.5 inches in Logan Square.  Chicago O'Hare Airport measured 4.4 inches.

A strong cold front moved across northern Illinois during the morning of January 14th with a band of heavy snow and snow squalls. Wind gusts were between 40 mph and 50 mph with a few higher gusts, including a gust to 61 mph near Crescent City. Visibility was reported to be near zero in many locations. Snowfall amounts were generally between 1 and 2 inches and with temperatures falling below freezing as the snow began, roads became icy.  Hundreds of vehicle accidents were reported across northern Illinois.

Wind gusts were between 40 mph and 50 mph with a few higher gusts, including a gust to 61 mph near Crescent City. Visibility was reported to be near zero in many locations. Snowfall amounts were generally between 1 and 2 inches and with temperatures falling below freezing as the snow began, roads became icy.  Hundreds of vehicle accidents were reported across northern Illinois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds advected warm moist air northward from Arkansas/Tennessee, resulting in dense fog across most of the southern two rows of counties in Southern Illinois early morning on the 8th. Daytime heating began to lower RHs below 100%, allowing for slow improvement in visibility mid-morning.

A light wintry mix developed during the early morning hours of the 21st across portions of southern Illinois. Temperatures were highly variable across the region between 32-36 degrees, but colder ground temperatures allowed for areas of light freezing rain to develop before temperatures quickly warmed that day, resulting in slippery and icy conditions on roadways. Roads were particularly bad near Glendale in Pope County, IL. Ice on rural roadways in Saline County, IL caused one vehicle to overturn with a person trapped. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

A cold air mass with sub-freezing temperatures that originated from Siberia was reinforced by a major winter storm that brought a snowpack across portions of southern Illinois on the 25th. Breezy west to northwest winds with occasional gusts around 20 mph caused dangerous intervals of wind chills between -15 to locally -18 in Williamson County, IL on the 26th. Northwest portions of southern Illinois and areas south of route 13 only experienced advisory criteria with values of -9 to -14. Dangerous wind chills eventually came to an end during the early morning hours of the 27th. The one exception was in Alexander, Pulaski, and Massac County, IL where wind chills warmed above -5 the morning of the 26th due to calmer winds.

For the sixth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits of 1 to 2 inches common during the month of January. Mean temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but below normal during December and January. While precipitation was below normal for the month, a disturbance brought almost an inch of rainfall west of I-55, which led to modest improvement in the drought conditions. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought expanded across central and eastern Illinois. Streamflows, lake levels, and regional water tables remained well below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a multi-month precipitation deficit dating back to the Fall of 2025, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. An early month disturbance brought almost an inch of rain, resulting in modest improvement in the drought by mid-January.

Breezy southerly winds shifted to southwesterly later into the evening of the 8th ahead of a cold frontal passage. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening hours in Southern Illinois. Gusty gradient winds of near and above 40mph developed in Southwestern Illinois, with a peak gust of 52mph at Southern Illinois Airport.
